data.title: Meta CEO Links Stock Drop To Higher Capex, Plans Team Cuts And More Apps: Report
data.description:
Key points:
  • Meta raised its 2026 capital expenditure forecast to between $125 billion and $145 billion, up from the previous range of $115 billion to $135 billion, on Wednesday.
  • Meta is looking to absorb some of its added CapEx costs by layoffs, including a 10% layoff planned for May.
  • Zuckerberg said on Thursday that the company will reduce the size of teams where AI can allow for greater speed and efficiency.

Meta Platforms (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg reportedly addressed employees in a companywide internal meeting on Thursday, attributing the stock’s sharp decline following the company’s first-quarter earnings to investor concerns over raised capital expenditure guidance and expectations of slower growth in the second quarter.

According to a report from The Wall Street Journal, Zuckerberg pinned the roughly 9% drop in share price on Thursday to those factors while maintaining that the company’s long-term trajectory remains very strong.

The CEO is now reportedly planning to reduce team sizes and build more apps.

Zuckerberg Addresses Q1 Earnings Reaction

“But I think Q1 was, like, really insanely strong and Q2 is merely strong, and I think the combination of that and the capex increase is what drove the near-term reaction. But I don’t know,” Zuckerberg reportedly told employees about the stock’s drop on Thursday while adding that he still sees a very strong trajectory for Meta’s businesses.

Meta raised its 2026 capital expenditure forecast to between $125 billion and $145 billion, up from the previous range of $115 billion to $135 billion, on Wednesday. Meta CFO Susan Li said the higher spending is driven in part by rising memory prices and additional data-center costs needed to support future capacity requirements.

Zuckerberg also highlighted challenges to the company's ad business in light of current geopolitical turmoil and highlighted that customers are less likely to purchase discretionary items when they are spending more on gas.

Layoffs And Team Reduction Efforts

Meta is looking to absorb some of its added CapEx costs by layoffs, including a 10% layoff planned for May. Investing more in compute and infrastructure means the company has less to allocate to its employees, the CEO said. “So that means that we do need to take down the size of the company somewhat,” Zuckerberg reportedly said.

He also said that the company will reduce the size of teams where AI can allow for greater speed and efficiency. However, a reduction in team size does not necessarily mean layoffs; affected employees will be reassigned to new projects, including building more apps.

“Historically, we’ve built like four or five big apps. We want to build a lot more apps, so there’s a bunch of stuff that we’re trying to figure out, and some of this we just need to figure out over time,” Zuckerberg reportedly said.

Zuckerberg is reportedly eyeing around 50 new apps, though he intends to start small.

Meta’s chief people officer, Janelle Gale, did not rule out the possibility of further layoffs at the meeting, the report said.

How Did META Retail Traders React?

On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around META increased from ‘bullish’ to ‘extremely bullish’ territory over the past 24 hours, while message volume stayed at ‘high’ levels.

META stock has added 11% over the past 12 months.

data.title: Liontown Lithium Recovery Expected to Improve — Market Talk
data.description: Liontown's lithium recovery should increase moving forward as underground ore makes up a higher proportion of its feed mix, Citi analyst Jack Whelan says. Reported 3Q recovery--averaging roughly 61%--missed consensus by 9%. That was because of a higher proportion of open pit stockpile material through the plant, Whelan says. Liontown "noted average recoveries of [circa] 70% have been achieved across the first three weeks of April," he says. Liontown is, however, yet to achieve its target grade of 5.2%, with 3Q grade averaging 5.1%, Whelan adds. Citi has a "neutral" rating and A$1.65 target on Liontown. Shares ended Thursday at A$2.35. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Tokyo April core CPI rises 1.5% yr/yr
data.description:
TOKYO, May 1 (Reuters) - Core consumer prices in Tokyo rose 1.5% in April from a year earlier, government data showed on Friday.

The core consumer price index for Japan’s capital, which includes oil products but excludes fresh food prices, compared with economists' median estimate for a 1.8% annual rise.

(Reporting by Tokyo Newsroom)

data.title: Apple's Incoming CEO John Ternus Makes Appearance as Company Bests Expectations — WSJ
data.description:
By Rolfe Winkler

Tim Cook's parting gift for his successor: strong iPhone sales and tidy margins.

Apple reported that revenue from iPhones rose 21.7% to nearly $57 billion in the second quarter compared with the prior year as customers, excited by the iPhone 17 lineup, continued to upgrade their devices at a brisk pace. That powered Apple to quarterly sales of $111.2 billion, results that exceeded Wall Street expectations.

The results might have been better if not for the constrained supply of advanced chips that power Apple's devices, said Apple Chief Financial Officer Kevan Parekh. Lately Taiwan Semiconductor, the maker of Apple's advanced chips, is selling more of its supply to artificial-intelligence chip makers such as Nvidia.

Apple's gross profit margin, which is closely watched by Wall Street, came in at 49.3%, also better than expectations. Feeding into strong profits: Services revenue that continues to outperform. It came in up 16.3% for the quarter, compared with a 14% increase that a consensus of analysts had predicted.

China was a bright spot for a second consecutive quarter after years of sluggish growth. IPhone sales there leapt 28%, following last quarter's 38% growth rate.

The strong sales gave Apple the top slot in global smartphone market share in the March quarter for the first time, according to research firm Counterpoint, which noted that Apple's position in the premium segment helped insulate it from skyrocketing memory and storage prices.

Cook, who noted this was his 89th earnings call, said September was the right moment to transition to CEO-in-waiting John Ternus, in part, because of the company's solid recent sales. "There's no one I trust more to lead," he said.

Ternus called it an "incredible honor" to follow in Cook's footsteps and touted Apple's product road map, but was coy about details.

Apple shares rose by more than 4% in after-hours trading.

Apple is also trying to weather — and even profit from — rising prices for memory and storage, key components in its devices. Higher prices reflect surging demand for the components from Nvidia and other AI companies, threatening the bottom line of all device makers.

Parekh deferred a question about rising memory costs, saying the company would address it during its earnings call later in the afternoon.

Higher memory costs are expected to hammer the smartphone market this year. Research firm IDC projects shipments to crash 13% in 2026, though it says the biggest impact will be felt by makers of cheap Android phones, whose devices are no longer profitable to produce. Apple and Samsung, which sell more expensive devices, will gain market share as a result, says IDC.

Indeed, Apple has gone on the offensive with aggressive pricing for entry-level devices of its own, the iPhone 17e and new MacBook Neo.

Ternus is set to take over as Apple CEO on Sept. 1, just before the company is expected to announce a new foldable iPhone to compete with rival models already on the market. The model should grab nearly half of the North American market for foldable smartphones for the year, according to Counterpoint's projections.

The biggest question for Ternus is whether he can turn around Apple's fortunes in AI. While the company makes money from fees that OpenAI kicks back for consumer subscriptions to the ChatGPT iPhone app, Apple's own AI offerings remain far behind the cutting edge, a black eye for the company that considers itself a leader in consumer technology.

Apple is expected to release a smarter version of its Siri digital assistant this year that it hopes will be the first step to turning around its AI fortunes.

Write to Rolfe Winkler at Rolfe.Winkler@wsj.com

data.title: Woolworths's Ability to Pass On Higher Costs Looks Constrained — Market Talk
data.description: Supermarket owner Woolworths is particularly constrained in its ability to pass on higher fuel costs and indirect inflation to customers, Macquarie says. That's because of its relative pricing perception. It also has to repair some trust issues with customers, Macquarie says. So, it assumes the headwinds that Woolworths is experiencing early in 4Q of FY 2026 linger into the following fiscal year. Macquarie cuts its EPS forecast for FY 2026 and FY 2027 by 2.4% and 5.7%, respectively. "The lift in inflation has occurred at unfortunate timing as Woolworths seeks to rebuild its value proposition/pricing trust with customers," Macquarie says. "However, top-line strength still points to relative resilience in an uncertain market." It retains a neutral on Woolworths. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: CYDY: Leronlimab shows robust ctDNA declines and tumor control in advanced colorectal cancer
data.description:
